stories.title: CPSC Recalls Wiifo Children's Tower Stools — Collapse and Entrapment Hazard

x_posts.label: Post 1
x_posts.text:
RECALL: Wiifo tower stools (model LT005) — ~9,700 sold on Amazon.

Can collapse or tip. A child's torso can fit through the sides.

Stop use now. Refund: support@wiifo.net.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
If you have a Wiifo learning tower at home — the kind that lets toddlers stand at counter height so they can help in the kitchen — it's time to check the model number.

The CPSC has recalled Wiifo-branded children's tower stools, model LT005, after 22 reported incidents of the stools collapsing and tipping over. Six children were injured. The hazard isn't just the fall: the openings on the sides of the stool are large enough for a child's torso to fit through, creating an entrapment risk in addition to the fall danger. About 9,700 units were sold on Amazon between June 2022 and March 2026 for about $60.

To identify the recalled product: look for "Model LT005" on a label on the underside of the standing platform. It comes in white, natural, and light wood finish.

If you have one, stop using it now. The remedy is a full refund — contact Wiifo at support@wiifo.net and disassemble the stool, then send a photo of the destroyed stool as proof.

These stools have become a go-to in a lot of homes with toddlers who want to be part of mealtime prep. Worth the few minutes to check.

Did you have one of these stools at home? Or have you heard about this recall from another parent?

---

#### Image Caption -- 8:00 AM MT
facebook.image_caption:
RECALL: 9,700 Wiifo children's tower stools recalled. Can collapse and trap kids.

If you have model LT005 — stop use now, full refund at support@wiifo.net.

stories.title: CPSC Recalls Vevor Baby Swings — Inclined Sleeper Ban Violation

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
RECALL: Vevor baby swings (Sanven Technology) violate the federal inclined sleeper ban.

Models BB501K, BB702A, BB005K. Sold on Amazon and Vevor.com, Jan–Aug 2025.

Stop use now. Contact: recalling@vevor.com.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Parents who bought a Vevor baby swing in 2025 — it's worth checking whether yours is on the recall list.

Sanven Technology has recalled three models of Vevor baby swings: BB501K (white with white base), BB702A (white with gray base), and BB005K (black with black base). The issue is a federal one: these swings have an incline greater than 10 degrees and violate the Safe Sleep for Babies Act, which bans inclined sleep products for infants. The incline creates a suffocation risk — the angle can cause an infant's head to tilt forward and block the airway.

The swings were sold on Vevor.com and Amazon.com from January 2025 through August 2025 for $65 to $80.

If you have one, stop using it now. To get a refund, remove the swing cover, cut it in half, and send a photo to recalling@vevor.com. You can also call 855-599-6320, Monday through Friday, 9 AM to 5 PM PT.

The inclined sleeper issue has been on the CPSC's radar since the Fisher-Price Rock 'n Play recall years ago, and the agency continues to act against products that don't meet the standard. If a swing or sleeper has been sitting in a closet since your youngest outgrew it, now is a good time to check what it is.

Have you seen this recall, or know of someone who has this product?

---

#### Image Caption -- 10:00 AM MT
facebook.image_caption:
RECALL: Vevor baby swings — inclined sleeper ban violation, suffocation risk.

Models BB501K, BB702A, BB005K. Sold 2025 on Amazon + Vevor.com. Stop use now.

stories.title: COS Families: Three Events Worth Checking Out This Week and Weekend

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
COS family events coming up:

May Faire, Mountain Song School — Sat May 2, 11 AM–2 PM
Star Party, Cheyenne Mountain — free telescope views this week
Old COS Memorial Day street festival — May 23-25

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
A few things worth putting on the calendar if you've got kids who need something to do this week or coming up.

This Saturday, May 2: Mountain Song Community School hosts its annual May Faire from 11 AM to 2 PM at 2904 W. Kiowa St. in Colorado Springs. Maypole dances, crown making, paper flower crafts, a gem dig, a butterfly activity, and a silent auction. It's been a community tradition for years — the kind of low-key Saturday event that Colorado Springs does well.

Also happening this week at Cheyenne Mountain State Park: the Colorado Springs Astronomical Society is setting up telescopes at the Limekiln Trailhead for a Star Party. If you've ever wanted to show your kids what a galaxy actually looks like through a telescope, this is a free way to do it. Worth checking the Cheyenne Mountain State Park calendar for the exact date and time.

Looking ahead: Old Colorado City hosts its annual Memorial Day weekend street festival May 23-25, with arts, crafts, food, and free entry. A solid family outing for the long weekend.

What are your COS family plans this week?

---

#### Image Caption -- 12:00 PM MT
facebook.image_caption:
May Faire at Mountain Song (May 2), Star Party at Cheyenne Mountain State Park this week, and Memorial Day weekend in Old Colorado City.

Some solid COS family plans coming up.

stories.title: AAP: Screen Time Limits Alone Are No Longer Enough — What Quality Means in 2026

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
New from the AAP: screen time isn't just about how long.

Kids watching with a parent show significantly better learning than those watching alone.

What they watch — and who's with them — matters more than the clock.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
The American Academy of Pediatrics shifted its screen time guidance in 2026, and it's worth knowing what changed — because it's actually more practical than what came before.

For years, the conversation was about limits: under 18 months, no screens. Ages 2-5, one hour per day. And while those limits haven't gone away, the AAP's updated framework is now clear that limits alone aren't the full story. A new report covered by ABC News puts it plainly: screen time limits are no longer enough.

What matters more, according to current research: what your child is watching, and whether an adult is watching with them. Kids who watch alongside a parent show significantly better learning transfer than kids watching alone. Co-viewing turns passive content into an opportunity for conversation, connection, and comprehension. That is a meaningful difference.

The flip side also holds: low-quality content — the kind that autoplays one video after another with no educational value and maximum stimulation — can disrupt sleep and attention regardless of how short the session is.

So the practical takeaway for Colorado Springs families isn't "watch less." It's "watch better, and watch together." That's a lot more achievable than it sounds.

How does your family handle screen time — do you watch with your kids, or is it more of a solo activity for them?

---

#### Image Caption -- 3:00 PM MT
facebook.image_caption:
New AAP guidance in 2026: it's not just how long your kids are on screens — it's what they're watching and whether you're watching with them.

Worth knowing.

article.headline: Screen Time Limits Aren't Enough Anymore — Here's What the AAP Says Matters More

research.facts:
Screen Time Quality > Quantity
**Sources consulted:**
- ABC News: "New report says screen time limits for children are no longer enough"
- CHOC Children's Health Hub: "Updated AAP recommendations for screen time"
- FSU News (Feb 2026): FSU psychologist guidance on new screen time recommendations

**Key verified facts:**
| Fact | Confidence | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| AAP 2026 position: quality, context, and conversation over strict time limits | HIGH | CHOC/AAP, ABC News |
| No screens before 18 months (unchanged) | HIGH | AAP |
| 1 hour high-quality content per day for ages 2-5 (unchanged) | HIGH | AAP |

stories.title: D11 Hoopster Basketball League Wraps Up — Championship Saturday May 9

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
D11 Hoopster Basketball Championship Saturday: May 9 at North Middle School.

96 COS students (4th and 5th grade) made the cut from 115+ who tried out. Regular play wraps May 2.

D11 last day of school: May 22.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
More than 115 Colorado Springs fourth and fifth graders tried out for the D11 Hoopster Basketball League this spring. Ninety-six made the cut.

Those 96 students have been playing divisional games every Saturday since April 18 at North Middle School, and the season wraps up with Championship Saturday on May 9. If you've got a connection to D11 elementary schools, this is a great Saturday to show up and cheer — 9 AM to 3 PM at North Middle School.

It's also a good reminder of where the school year sits. D11's last day of school is May 22, which puts us at less than four weeks from summer break. Championship Saturday is one of the last big community events before that countdown reaches zero.

Whether your family is in the Hoopster league or not, it's the kind of community event that COS does well — neighborhood kids competing, families in the bleachers, school year coming to a close.

Will you be at Championship Saturday on May 9?

---

#### Image Caption -- 5:00 PM MT
facebook.image_caption:
D11 Hoopster Basketball Championship Saturday is May 9 at North Middle School.

96 COS students made it through tryouts. Come support them.
